data.title: Celestica: A Likely Q3 Beat May Not Spark Another Rally
data.description:
I'd Not Buy Celestica Before Its Upcoming Q3 Release

I know that many bulls associate strong earnings beats with subsequent rallies, and in most cases, it does happen this way: a company shows a double-beat, and then its stock price updates to a new ATH in a matter of days.

But it's not always working like that.

Celestica's (NYSE:CLS) Q4 2024, for example, was a double-beating quarter, just like all other quarters since late FY2021. But it failed to be in line with the magnitude of the previous quarters - the EPS and revenue beats were only 0.48% and 5.13%, respectively, which was below the average of the past beats, and so the market punished the stock price quite meaningfully before CLS reported for its Q1 2025 and proved that its business momentum isn't going anywhere.

I know that the above price action of early 2025 was superimposed on the broader market's weakness, which only exacerbated the downturn, but still, Celestica's meltdown of over 44% followed its great quarterly results, and there were limited signs of fundamental weakness that could have explained that move.

It seems to me now that CLS is going through the same path, and the stock price may start to consolidate for some time amid all the positivity we're seeing regarding its forward sales and earnings growth rates.

As a long-term investor, I'll be holding CLS because its margin expansion amid the revenue mix shift isn't done yet. But the stock is looking too pricey to justify a "Buy" rating at its current price levels, and that's why I'd not buy CLS ahead of its Q3 print (expected in less than 3 weeks).

Why Do I Think So?

In my previous article on CLS, which generally reviewed the firm's Q2 a few days after the release date, I downgraded the stock to "Hold" because, despite strong consolidated revenue of $2.89 billion and adjusted EPS of $1.39 (both exceeded guidance), I saw that the post-earnings move-up basically erased the forward potential for buyers. I was a bit premature; now I see it after CLS's continued rally and addition of another 28%+. But I also see that after the analysts rushed to upgrade their Q3 and Q4 EPS and sales estimates following the management's guidance raise, most of the future business momentum got priced in.

For example, Wall Street analysts are expecting Celestica's EPS to be ~$1.47 (+41.09% YoY) with revenues of ~$3.02 billion (+20.72% YoY).

Effectively, it's $20 million and 2 cents above the midpoints of the revenue and EPS guidance figures the management shared for Q3, respectively.

For Q3 2025, revenue is projected to be between $2.875 billion and $3.125 billion, with adjusted EPS anticipated between $1.37 and $1.53. Chawla indicated a non-GAAP operating margin outlook of 7.4% at the midpoint.

Source: Seeking Alpha Earnings Insights on CLS's Q2

In the past, CLS used to beat the high ends of its internal guidance ranges in terms of EPS almost in every single quarter. In Q2 2025, the closest example, they reported an EPS of $1.39, which was way bigger than the higher end of the initial guidance ($1.27). For Q1, they also reported above the high end of the range. The deeper I go, the clearer I see that Celestica managed to beat its EPS guidance high ends in every single quarter throughout fiscal 2024, and it couldn't help but lead to the continued rally in the stock price.

So, it's not going to be something extraordinary for CLS to show another high-end beating quarter in Q3. Assuming a slight 2-5% premium on top of the highest end of the Q3 EPS guidance, I get an implied EPS of $1.56, which is a beat of over 6.1-9.2%. While looking pretty solid, that beating potential would be a bit lower than the average historical beat since 2022, based on Seeking Alpha data.

Within Celestica's business structure, its CCS is the key segment, and within that segment, the high-performance solutions (HPS) sub-segment is the one that is currently changing the whole business model, and it's the key to the revenue mix shift, which can prospectively bring in higher margins. It's still driving the consolidated marginality higher. But the difference, the gap between CCS's operating margin and ATS's one, is starting to look minimal. Even with HPS gaining the largest chunk within CCS, the CCS operating margin of 8.3% is less than 100 bps larger than the consolidated margin of 7.4%. Given that CLS won't be able to reach 8.3% on a consolidated basis unless a) ATS is gone or b) the CCS gains a massive margin expansion itself, which doesn't seem to be the case, the past EPS growth of 30-40% is going to slow down dramatically at one point.

The demand environment is pretty lucrative for CLS, I agree, and it makes me bullish in the long run. That's why I'm not selling the stock. But I'm getting concerned that the high multiples that the stock received for the firm's ability to turn even minimal margin expansion into massive double-digit growth momentum is going to take a hit once that ability weakens.

We see that compared to the majority of its peers, Celestica is growing its earnings at much higher rates, but starting in 2026, the momentum is set to slow down dramatically (for FY2027, I see a slowdown of 2x in terms of YoY EPS changes).

So, with that in mind, I think that at 30x earnings next year, and with a premium of 5% on top of the FY2026 high-end consensus estimate of $8.6, which is already above the base-case consensus estimate, I get a price target of $270.9/share. This PT is only 5-6% above the current stock price, so it's a reason for me to leave my previous "Hold" rating in place before Celestica's Q3 comes out.

Most analysts on the Street have a much gloomier outlook when it comes to calculating Celestica's forward price target. I politely don't agree with their consensus target itself, but I concur that the upside may already be capped.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: BETA Technologies Begins IPO Takeoff Plan
data.description:
BETA Technologies Is In The Late-ish Certification Stage

BETA Technologies, Inc. (BETA) has filed to raise $100 million in an IPO of its Class A common stock, according to SEC S-1 registration information.

BETA is developing all-electric airplanes and related technologies in the United States, Europe, and the Middle East.

The firm is still in the development/certification stage and preparing for an early commercialization stage, likely in 2027.

The stock may catch the eye of 'narrative' traders/investors, so volatility in open market trading may be significant.

What Does BETA Technologies Do?

South Burlington, Vermont-based BETA Technologies, Inc. was founded to develop all-electric airplanes for passenger and cargo operations and related charging technologies in the US and overseas.

Management is led by founder, President and CEO Mr. Kyle Clark, who founded the firm in June 2018 and was previously co-founder of iTherm Technologies and Director of Engineering at Dynapower Company.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • CTOL Airplane.

  • VTOL Airplane.

  • Electric Aircraft Components.

  • Charging and Support Equipment.

  • Beta Charge Network.

The firm's CTOL aircraft has flown nearly 83,000 nautical miles, and the company has operations in the US, Europe, and the Middle East.

BETA intends to generate revenue from aircraft sales, battery sales, propulsion system sales, and charging equipment sales.

BETA's proprietary charge network footprint, 52 Active and 32 in Progress, is shown in the graphic here:

The current backlog of orders for its ALIA CTOL aircraft "consists of 331 units, of which 131 units are for Firm Orders and 200 units are for Options…We are targeting FAA Part 23 certification at the end of 2026 or early 2027."

The backlog for its ALIA VTOL aircraft is 560 units, "of which 158 units are for Firm Orders and 402 units are for Options, with a goal of achieving type certification ('Type Certification') towards the end of 2027 or early 2028".

BETA's current revenue contribution is shown in the pie charts below:

BETA Technologies has booked fair market value investment of $1.44 billion as of June 30, 2025, from investors, including TPG (FKA Texas Pacific Group), Charles Davis, John E. Abele, Amazon NV Investment Holdings, General Electric Company (GE), and Austin Meyer.

GE Aerospace is investing up to $300 million in BETA in conjunction with an agreement to co-develop a hybrid electric turbogenerator designed for long-range flight operations that may also be suited for defense environments.

General & Administrative expenses as a percentage of total revenue have varied as revenues have remained low, as the figures below indicate:

General & Administrative - TTM

Expenses vs. Revenue

Period

Percentage

Six Mos. Ended June 30, 2025

347.4%

2024

502.8%

2023

401.3%

(Source: SEC).

The General & Administrative efficiency multiple, i.e., how many dollars of additional revenue are produced from each dollar of General & Administrative expense, was 0.1x in the most recent reporting period, as shown in the table below:

General & Administrative - TTM

Efficiency Rate

Period

Multiple

Six Mos. Ended June 30, 2025

0.1

2024

0.0

(Source: SEC).

The company faces high customer concentration risks and geographic concentration risks.

What Is BETA Technologies' Market?

According to a 2025 market research report by Precedence Research, the global electric aircraft market is an estimated $13.7 billion in 2025 and is expected to grow to over $74 billion by 2034.

This represents a projected CAGR of 20.65% from 2025 to 2034, a very high forecasted growth rate.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are strong demand for environmentally improved forms of transportation in North America and overseas and continued technological advancements from vendors.

However, inadequate existing infrastructure and high costs will slow development somewhat in the coming years.

Also, the chart below shows the projected growth trajectory of the electric aircraft market through 2034:

Major competitive vendors or industry players include the following firms:

  • Archer Aviation Inc. (ACHR).

  • Joby Aviation, Inc. (JOBY).

  • Eviation Aircraft.

  • Airbus SE (OTCPK:EADSF).

  • Lilium N.V. (OTC:LILMF).

  • The Boeing Company (BA).

  • Pipistrel Group.

  • Aerospace Limited.

  • EHang Holdings Limited (EH).

  • Yuneec Holding.

  • Leonardo S.p.a. (OTCPK:FINMF).

  • Faradair Aerospace.

  • Eve Air Mobility.

  • Vertical Aerospace.

  • Digisky.

  • Groupe Gorge.

Archer and Joby are perhaps the most relevant competitors to BETA in the eVTOL/urban air mobility/air taxi space, and at the stage of development, while Airbus and Boeing are legacy aerospace firms with deep certification and regulatory expertise.

Airbus, Lilium, Pipistrel, Leonardo, Faradair, Digisky and Groupe Gorge are all based in Europe, so they may have regulatory and market penetration advantages if they can achieve approvals and/or obtain enough financing.

EHang and Yuneec are prominent electric aircraft and components makers based in China.

The firm seeks customers for its products from among government and commercial customers in the US and Europe.

BETA currently has subsidiaries in Canada, Ireland and the UAE for R&D purposes and will pursue commercialization efforts in countries depending on its resources and opportunities.

I've prepared a thumbnail SWOT analysis for BETA Technologies:

Strengths

  • Vertically integrated technology and business model, large IP portfolio, strong customer relationships from marquee clients, and established manufacturing footprint.

Weaknesses

  • No certified aircraft deliveries yet, high capital intensity, and limited operating history.

Opportunities

  • Large market growth potential, recurring revenue opportunities, and first-mover advantage in infrastructure.

Threats

  • Dependent on aircraft certification in the short term, growing competition, reliance on external suppliers for battery cells, and production scaling risks in the longer term.

BETA Technologies' Recent Financial Results

The firm's recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Variable topline revenue.

  • Fluctuating gross profit and gross margin.

  • Heavy operating losses and cash used in operations.

Below are major financial results from the firm's current registration statement:

Total Revenue
